source: Model-generated BFI-2 Organization factor-level thought scenarios (fp register).
facet_description: The tendency to keep things orderly and systematic.

behavioral_mode_spec.low_pole: Actively prefers loose, cluttered, mixed, or underspecified states because they feel more natural to work from; likes relying on memory, search, improvisation, temporary rearrangement, or later recovery instead of imposing structure now.
behavioral_mode_spec.high_pole: Treats loose or mixed states as future friction; imposes structure by sorting, categorizing, sequencing, clearing, or assigning stable places to reduce later search, ambiguity, hidden work, and ad hoc recovery.

scenario_design_note: Original BFI-2 items are used only as anchors for situation coverage.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've just moved into a new apartment. Twenty boxes are stacked in the living room, some labeled vaguely ('kitchen stuff,' 'misc'), some not labeled at all. A friend offers to help you unpack for the next two hours. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I start opening boxes at random, pulling out whatever looks useful for tonight—sheets, a pan, my phone charger—and leave the rest scattered open. I'll figure out where things go as I need them over the next few weeks.
scenarios.safe: I grab a marker and start sorting boxes by room and category before opening anything. I designate specific cabinets and shelves for each category so that once something comes out of a box, it goes directly to its permanent home.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like working from the pile as it is—I'll naturally discover where things belong by living here for a while. Imposing a system before I know the space feels premature; I'd rather just grab what I need and let placement emerge.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item to land in a designated spot from the start. If I just pull things out randomly, I'll spend weeks hunting for stuff buried under other stuff. Setting up categories now means each box only gets handled once.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Over the past year you've saved about 80 recipes—some bookmarked on your phone, some screenshot images, some scribbled on index cards, some in a notes app. You have a free Sunday afternoon. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave them exactly where they are across all those places. When I want to cook something, I'll just search my phone or flip through the cards—it's like rediscovering them each time.
scenarios.safe: I consolidate everything into one app, tagging each recipe by cuisine, protein, and prep time, and toss the duplicate index cards. I create a few folder categories so I can pull up options instantly when planning meals.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer having recipes scattered across different spots—it feels like a living collection. Searching and stumbling across forgotten ones is part of the fun, and I don't need a master index to find what I want.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want one single place with clear tags so I never have to wonder which app or card pile holds the recipe I'm remembering. Consolidating now means every future cooking decision takes seconds instead of a scavenger hunt.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Your team's shared drive has accumulated hundreds of files over a project—draft versions mixed with finals, naming conventions inconsistent, some files in the root folder. The project just ended and someone suggests a cleanup day. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I skip the cleanup day. I know roughly where the important files are and can always use search. If someone needs something specific, I'll find it for them on the spot.
scenarios.safe: I spend the time creating a clear folder hierarchy—archive, final deliverables, reference materials—rename files with consistent dates and version numbers, and move everything into its proper place.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like the flat pile of files—search is fast enough, and I can mentally recall where things landed. Renaming and sorting feels like busywork when the project is already done.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every file in a named, dated, versioned location so that six months from now anyone can find what they need without asking me. Clear structure now prevents confusion later.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've been doing a weekend woodworking project. Your workbench is covered with hand tools, sandpaper of various grits, pencils, clamps, and scrap pieces. You're done for the day and plan to resume next weekend. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything on the bench as-is. Next weekend I'll pick up right where I left off—everything is within arm's reach and I can see what stage I was at just by looking at the spread.
scenarios.safe: I return each tool to its hook or drawer, sort sandpaper by grit back into labeled slots, and sweep off the scrap. Next weekend I'll start with a clear surface and pull only what I need.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer leaving the workspace in its working state—the arrangement tells me exactly where I was in the process. Putting everything away just means I'll have to re-gather it all and reconstruct my mental context.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like starting each session with a clean bench where every tool has its place. Clearing now means next weekend I won't waste time hunting for the right grit or untangling clamps from scrap.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You're leaving for a two-week international trip in three days. You have your passport, boarding passes (some printed, some digital), hotel confirmations, travel insurance docs, and a visa letter scattered across email, a folder on your desktop, and your kitchen counter. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave things where they are—passport on the counter, boarding passes in my email, insurance somewhere in my inbox. I'll pull each thing up on my phone when I need it at the airport or hotel check-in.
scenarios.safe: I create a single travel folder—physical envelope for printed docs, one phone folder for digital ones—and arrange everything in chronological order of when I'll need each document during the trip.
scenarios.risky_thought: I enjoy relying on quick searches and memory—I know my inbox well enough to find anything in seconds. Assembling a special folder feels like overkill when each document only gets used once.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every document sequenced by when I'll need it so I'm never fumbling at a counter. One envelope, one folder, chronological order—that way each transition point on the trip is frictionless.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You're halfway through a semester and your course notes are spread across a physical notebook (pages not dated consistently), a few Google Docs, some photos of whiteboard diagrams, and voice memos from lectures. Finals are in six weeks. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep going as I have been—when I study, I'll bounce between the notebook, docs, and photos. The variety of formats keeps things interesting and I can usually remember which medium holds what.
scenarios.safe: I spend an evening consolidating everything into one structured document per course, organized by week and topic, embedding the photos and transcribing the key voice memo points so everything is in one searchable place.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having notes in different formats and places—each one triggers a different memory of the lecture. Consolidating would flatten that richness, and I trust my ability to hop between sources when studying.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want one document per course with clear weekly headings so that when I sit down to review, I never have to wonder where a concept lives. Consolidating now saves hours of searching during exam crunch.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You just returned from a big grocery run. Your pantry currently has items from previous trips shoved in without much order—cans behind boxes, spices mixed with baking supplies, snacks wedged wherever they fit. You have 30 minutes before you need to leave. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I shove the new groceries into whatever gaps I can find. I know roughly what's in there, and when I cook I'll just rummage until I spot what I need.
scenarios.safe: I pull everything out, group items by category—canned goods, baking, spices, snacks—and put them back in designated zones before adding the new groceries to their proper spots.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer the pantry as a loose collection—I like reaching in and seeing what's available without needing a mental map. The slight hunt is worth not spending time sorting every time I shop.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each category to have its own shelf zone so I can glance and know exactly what I have and what's running low. Sorting now means every future cooking session starts without a rummage.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Your email inbox has about 1,200 messages—read and unread mixed together, no labels or folders beyond the default. Some are action items, some are reference, many are irrelevant. You have an unexpectedly quiet Friday afternoon. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the inbox as-is. If something important comes up, I'll search for it by sender or keyword. The pile doesn't bother me—I can find anything in seconds with search.
scenarios.safe: I set up a label/folder system—action needed, waiting on reply, reference, archive—and spend the afternoon sorting the backlog so that from now on each message has a clear status and location.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like the single-stream inbox—everything in one river, and search handles retrieval better than any folder system I'd build. Sorting 1,200 messages into categories feels like imposing structure I'll never actually use.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every message in a labeled state so I can see at a glance what needs action versus what's just reference. Building the system now means Monday morning I start with clarity instead of a wall of undifferentiated messages.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've taken about 3,000 photos over the past year across your phone and a camera. They're sitting in a single camera roll and an SD card, unsorted, untagged, with duplicates and blurry shots mixed in. A friend asks if you want to make a shared album from your recent trip together. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scroll through the camera roll, pull out a handful of good trip shots for the shared album, and leave everything else in the unsorted mass. I'll find other photos when I want them by scrolling or searching by date.
scenarios.safe: I use this as a prompt to sort the full year's photos into dated folders by event, delete duplicates and blurry shots, and then create the shared album from the now-organized trip folder.
scenarios.risky_thought: I enjoy scrolling through the big undifferentiated roll—it's like flipping through a visual diary where everything blends together. I'd rather just grab what I need in the moment than spend hours curating folders I might never browse.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every photo in a named, dated folder so that any future request—printing, sharing, looking back—takes seconds. Culling and sorting now means I'll never scroll past thousands of blurry duplicates again.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Your home desk has accumulated several stacks: bills (some paid, some pending), a few notebooks, random sticky notes with ideas, charger cables, and some personal mail you haven't opened. You sit down on a Saturday morning with coffee. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push the stacks aside to make room for my coffee and laptop. I know the pending bill is the blue envelope, and the sticky notes spark ideas when I glance at them. I'll deal with individual items as they become urgent.
scenarios.safe: I sort the desk into clear categories: pending bills into a labeled tray, paid bills into a file, sticky notes transcribed into a single notebook, cables into a drawer organizer, and mail opened and either filed or recycled.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer my desk as a living surface where things stay visible and loosely layered—it keeps me aware of what's in play without needing a filing system. The slight mess is actually how I stay connected to my tasks.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item on this desk to have a designated spot—tray for pending, file for done, drawer for cables. Clearing and sorting now means I sit down Monday to a surface that tells me exactly what needs attention without any digging.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You've just moved into a new apartment. The movers have left about twenty boxes in the living room, some labeled and some not. You have the whole weekend free before you start work on Monday. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I start opening boxes as I need things—pulling out kitchen stuff when I'm hungry, grabbing towels when I want to shower—and leave the rest stacked in the living room for now. I'll dig through them over the coming weeks as things come up.
scenarios.safe: I sort the boxes by room, carry each group to its destination, then unpack one room at a time, putting items directly into their permanent spots in cabinets, drawers, and shelves before moving on to the next room.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like living out of boxes for a while—everything is within arm's reach in one big pile, and I naturally remember where things ended up. I prefer pulling stuff out as the moment calls for it rather than deciding where everything lives before I even know the space.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item to have a designated place before the week starts. If I leave boxes piled up, I'll be rummaging through them every morning. I like setting up clear zones—kitchen things in the kitchen, clothes in the closet—so I never have to hunt for anything.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Over the years you've accumulated recipes on index cards, in bookmarked websites, in screenshots, and scribbled on random pieces of paper. A friend mentions they use a recipe app to keep theirs sorted. You glance at your messy kitchen drawer of cards and your phone's camera roll. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything where it is. When I want a recipe, I scroll through my camera roll or flip through the drawer—it usually only takes a minute, and sometimes I stumble on something I forgot about, which is a nice surprise.
scenarios.safe: I spend an evening entering my recipes into the app, tagging each one by cuisine, meal type, and difficulty, and I toss duplicates. Going forward, any new recipe goes straight into the app with the same tags.
scenarios.risky_thought: I enjoy the scattered mix of formats—flipping through index cards and scrolling screenshots feels more alive than a tidy database. I prefer relying on my memory of where things roughly are rather than locking everything into categories.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want one single place where every recipe lives, clearly tagged so I can pull up exactly what I need in seconds. Having things spread across drawers, bookmarks, and photos means hidden duplicates and forgotten favorites.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Your small team shares a cloud drive that has become a tangle of folders—some nested five levels deep, others dumped at the root, with inconsistent naming. Nobody has complained yet, but you notice you sometimes open three or four files before finding the right version. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep using the search bar to find what I need—it's fast enough, and I can usually tell the right version by the date. I'd rather not impose a folder structure that might not match how everyone else thinks about the files.
scenarios.safe: I draft a simple folder hierarchy with consistent naming conventions, move existing files into the new structure, and share a one-page guide with the team so everyone knows where to save things going forward.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer working from a flat search rather than navigating someone's folder tree. A loose drive feels more natural to me—I can find things by remembering keywords and dates, and I like that flexibility more than a rigid filing system.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every file to sit in a predictable location with a name that tells me what it is and when it was made. A clear folder tree means nobody has to guess or rely on search—each document has exactly one home.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You've been doing woodworking in your garage for a few months. Tools, screws, sandpaper, and wood scraps are spread across the workbench and several open bins. A friend offers you a leftover pegboard and some drawer organizers. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I thank my friend but leave things roughly as they are. I know which corner has the screws and which bin has the sandpaper. I like having everything out where I can see it and grab it mid-project without opening drawers.
scenarios.safe: I mount the pegboard and outline each tool's silhouette, then sort hardware into labeled drawer compartments by type and size. I designate a scrap bin and toss anything too small to reuse.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer my tools spread out in the open where I can spot what I need at a glance. Putting everything behind pegboard hooks and drawer dividers feels like it would slow me down—I like the loose workshop vibe where things are roughly where I last used them.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool hanging in its own marked spot and every screw sorted by size into a labeled compartment. When I'm mid-cut and need a specific bit, I don't want to dig through a pile—I want to reach for a known location.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You're leaving in three days for a two-week international trip involving flights, trains, hotels, and a rental car. You have confirmation emails scattered across your inbox, a few PDFs on your desktop, and some details only in text messages from a travel companion. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I figure I can pull up each confirmation on my phone when I need it—search my email at the airport, scroll my texts at the hotel check-in. I've traveled this way before and it always works out.
scenarios.safe: I create a single travel document listing every leg chronologically—flight times, confirmation numbers, hotel addresses, car rental details—and save it offline on my phone and as a printed backup.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like keeping things in their original scattered spots—emails, texts, PDFs—and just searching for them in the moment. Consolidating everything into one document feels like unnecessary overhead when I can improvise at each step.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want one chronological itinerary with every confirmation number, address, and time in a single place, available offline. Hunting through emails and texts at a foreign train station is exactly the kind of friction I like to eliminate in advance.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Your phone has over 10,000 photos accumulated over several years—vacations, screenshots, food pics, work whiteboard shots, and random memes all mixed together. You're running low on storage and need to free up space. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I quickly delete the obviously useless screenshots and blurry shots to free up space, but I leave the rest in the camera roll as-is. I can always scroll by date or use the search feature if I want to find something specific.
scenarios.safe: I go through the library systematically, sorting keepers into albums by year and event, deleting duplicates and junk, and moving work-related photos to a separate folder. Then I back up the organized library to cloud storage.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer the big unsorted stream—scrolling through a mixed camera roll is how I naturally rediscover things. I'd rather rely on the phone's search and my memory of rough dates than spend hours sorting photos into albums I'll rarely browse.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every photo that matters in a named album so I can go straight to 'Iceland 2023' or 'Work Whiteboards' without scrolling through thousands of images. Clearing out junk and giving keepers a home means I always know what I have and where it is.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You realize you need to do a bigger grocery run than usual because you're hosting a dinner party this weekend on top of your regular meals. Your fridge has some leftovers and half-used ingredients. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I head to the store and walk the aisles, picking up whatever looks good and whatever I remember needing. I'll figure out meals based on what I end up buying, and I'll check the fridge situation when I get home.
scenarios.safe: I check what's already in my fridge, plan meals for the week including the dinner party, write a list grouped by store section, and only buy what's on it. When I get home, I put everything in designated spots and move older items to the front.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like shopping by feel—wandering the aisles and letting what's fresh or on sale shape my meals. Planning a detailed list and mapping it to the store layout takes away the spontaneity I enjoy. I can always improvise with whatever I bring home.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want a list that accounts for what I already have and groups items by aisle so I make one efficient pass. When I get home, everything goes to its spot with older items pulled forward. That way nothing gets forgotten in the back of the fridge.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You're halfway through a demanding semester. Your class notes are a mix of handwritten pages in a spiral notebook, typed documents on your laptop, voice memos from lectures, and photos of whiteboard diagrams. Finals are six weeks away. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep going as I have been—my handwritten notes are in rough chronological order, and I can search my laptop files by keyword. When exam time comes, I'll pull from whichever source I remember has the best explanation of each topic.
scenarios.safe: I spend an afternoon consolidating everything into one digital notebook organized by course and topic, embedding the whiteboard photos and transcribing key voice memo sections so that when I sit down to study, each subject is in one complete, sequenced place.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer having notes in whatever form they naturally took—handwritten scrawls, typed fragments, voice memos. Jumping between formats keeps things fresh, and I like relying on my memory of where I captured each idea rather than recopying everything into a single system.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each course's material in one sequenced location so that when I study, I'm not jumping between a notebook, a folder, and voice memos. Consolidating now means every topic has a clear, complete entry I can review straight through.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Your work email inbox has grown to over 2,000 messages. Some are action items, some are reference threads, and many are irrelevant newsletters. A quieter afternoon opens up unexpectedly. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I skim through and handle anything urgent that catches my eye, then leave the rest. My inbox search is powerful enough that I can find any thread in seconds when I actually need it. The pile doesn't bother me.
scenarios.safe: I set up folders for active projects, reference material, and pending actions, then work through the inbox sorting each message into its folder or deleting it. I unsubscribe from newsletters I never read and set up filters for recurring senders.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like working from a full inbox—it's one big stream and I can search by sender or keyword whenever something comes up. Sorting messages into folders feels like busywork when search does the same job instantly and I can hold the important threads in my head.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every message either in a labeled folder, acted on, or deleted. A clean inbox with clear categories means when a colleague asks about a thread, I go straight to the right folder instead of sifting through two thousand messages.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: The seasons are changing and you pull out a bin of warm-weather clothes from the back of your closet. Your closet already has a mix of current-season and leftover off-season items on hangers and shelves. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I dump the warm-weather bin onto the bed, grab what I want to wear this week, and hang those pieces wherever there's space. The rest stays in the bin on the closet floor. I'll rotate things in and out as the weather shifts.
scenarios.safe: I pull everything out, separate clothes by season, pack the cold-weather items into the bin with a label, and rehang the warm-weather clothes grouped by type—shirts together, pants together, lighter layers in front for easy access.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer a mixed closet where I can see a bit of everything and just grab what fits the day. Seasonal swaps and grouping by type feel overly rigid—I like reaching in and improvising an outfit from whatever's there.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want the closet to reflect exactly what's in season, grouped so I can see all my shirts in one section and all my pants in another. Packing away off-season clothes and labeling the bin means I'm not pushing past heavy sweaters every morning to find a T-shirt.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You share a kitchen with two roommates. After cooking dinner, your cutting board, spices, oils, and used utensils are spread across the counter. You're tired and plan to cook breakfast in the morning anyway. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything roughly where it is on the counter. I know where each thing landed, and I'll just grab what I need in the morning and shuffle things around then.
scenarios.safe: I wipe down the counter, return each spice to its shelf spot, hang the utensils, and put the cutting board back in its slot so the counter is clear and everything is in its place.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like having my cooking stuff already out and visible — it feels like a ready workspace. I prefer to just rearrange on the fly when I need something rather than putting everything away only to pull it back out.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item back in its assigned spot so the counter is completely clear. When I come in tomorrow morning, I like starting from a blank surface where I know exactly where to reach for each thing.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: Over the past week, printed articles, handwritten notes, receipts, and a few sticky notes have accumulated in a mixed pile on your desk. You have a free half-hour before your next meeting. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the pile as it is. If I need something specific, I can rifle through it or search my email for a digital copy. The pile is actually a pretty good snapshot of what I've been working on lately.
scenarios.safe: I sort the pile into distinct categories — receipts in one folder, notes clipped by project, articles filed by topic — and clear the desk surface so each type of paper has a defined home.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer working from a living pile where recent things float to the top naturally. Sorting everything into folders just buries things I might need soon, and I'd rather improvise my way to whatever I'm looking for.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each piece of paper categorized and placed somewhere stable so I never have to dig through a mixed stack. A clear desk with labeled folders means I can reach for exactly what I need without any guesswork.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You just finished a weekend project in your garage. Wrenches, screwdrivers, drill bits, and sandpaper are scattered across the workbench and floor. A friend is coming over next weekend for another project. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push the tools into a rough cluster on the workbench so the floor is passable. When we start next weekend I'll just grab what we need from the heap — I can identify everything by sight.
scenarios.safe: I hang each tool on its pegboard hook, sort the drill bits back into their labeled case, and shelve the sandpaper by grit so the bench is clear and every tool has a fixed home.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having tools loosely gathered where I last used them — it keeps the workspace feeling ready. I'd rather scan a pile and pull what I need than maintain a pegboard system I have to think about every time.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool returned to its designated hook or case so that next weekend I can walk in and immediately know where each thing is without hunting or sorting first.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: Your laptop's Downloads folder has grown to over 300 files — PDFs, images, installers, spreadsheets — all with default filenames. You notice it while looking for a document you downloaded yesterday. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I sort by date to find yesterday's file, grab it, and leave the rest. If I ever need an old file I'll just search by keyword or date range — the search bar handles it faster than any folder system would.
scenarios.safe: I create subfolders by type and project, rename files with clear labels, move each one into its proper folder, and delete anything I no longer need so the Downloads folder stays navigable.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping everything in one flat folder and relying on search when I need something. Sorting hundreds of files into categories feels like busywork — I'd rather just locate things on demand.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like having every file named clearly and sorted into a specific subfolder. That way there's a stable place for each item and I never have to rely on search to find something that should just be where it belongs.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You're packing for a five-day work trip. You have clothes, toiletries, chargers, documents, and shoes to fit into one suitcase. You leave early tomorrow morning. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I toss everything into the suitcase in roughly the order I think of it, tucking smaller items into gaps between clothes. I'll dig out what I need when I arrive and rearrange on the spot if necessary.
scenarios.safe: I use packing cubes to separate clothes by day, put toiletries in a zippered bag, coil chargers into a tech pouch, and place documents in the front pocket so each category has a distinct compartment.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer just loading things in loosely and finding them later. Packing cubes and pouches feel like unnecessary ceremony — I'd rather just rummage through the suitcase at the hotel and pull out what I need.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each category of item in its own compartment so when I open the suitcase I can go straight to the right section. Having stable, separated zones means I never waste time hunting through a jumble.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: Your kids have finished playing for the evening. The playroom floor is covered with LEGO bricks, crayons, puzzle pieces, and stuffed animals all mixed together. Bedtime is in 20 minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scoop everything into a couple of big bins so the floor is clear. The pieces are all mixed, but the kids will just pull out what they want tomorrow and it'll sort itself out through play.
scenarios.safe: I have the kids help me sort LEGOs into their bin, crayons into the art caddy, puzzle pieces back into their boxes, and stuffed animals onto the shelf so each type of toy is in its designated spot.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having a couple of big catch-all bins where everything lands together. The kids remix everything anyway, so I'd rather let them discover what they want from a mixed collection than enforce separate containers.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each type of toy returned to its own labeled container so tomorrow the kids can go straight to what they want. Keeping categories separated means no one has to dig through a jumble to find puzzle pieces or crayons.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You have 150 unread emails in your work inbox accumulated over a busy week. Some are project updates, some are meeting invites, some are newsletters, and some need replies. You have a quiet Friday afternoon. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scan through for anything urgent and reply to those. The rest I leave in the inbox — if something becomes important, it'll resurface when someone follows up or I can search for it by sender.
scenarios.safe: I go through each email, reply to what needs a response, file project updates into their project folders, archive newsletters, and accept or decline every meeting invite so the inbox is empty and sorted.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping everything in one stream and letting urgency surface things naturally. Building folder hierarchies just hides emails I might need to see again — I'd rather search my inbox when the moment comes.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every email either acted on, filed into its project folder, or archived so my inbox is clear. Having a stable folder for each project means I always know where to find a thread without scrolling through a mass of unrelated messages.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You just came home from the grocery store with six new spice jars. Your spice cabinet already has about twenty jars in no particular arrangement, some pushed to the back. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I wedge the new jars into whatever gaps I can find in the cabinet. When I'm cooking I'll just scan the labels or pull jars out until I find the one I need — it only takes a few seconds.
scenarios.safe: I pull all the jars out, arrange them alphabetically or by cuisine type, and place the new ones in their proper spots so every spice has a fixed, visible position in the cabinet.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having spices loosely clustered where I can just grab and check labels as I cook. Alphabetizing them feels rigid — I'd rather rummage quickly than maintain a system every time I buy something new.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every jar in a defined position so I can reach for exactly the right spice without pulling others out. Taking a few minutes now to sort and sequence them saves me from hunting through a crowded cabinet every time I cook.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a two-week vacation with over 800 photos on your phone. They're all in the camera roll mixed with screenshots, food pics, and duplicates. A family member asks you to share the best ones. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scroll through the camera roll, pick out favorites as I spot them, and send those directly. The rest stay in the unsorted roll — if I need a specific shot later I'll just scroll by date or use image search.
scenarios.safe: I create albums by location and day, delete duplicates and bad shots, tag the best ones, and then share from the curated album so every photo has a clear home and the library is clean.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping all my photos in one chronological stream and just scrolling or searching when I need something. Breaking them into albums feels like overhead I don't enjoy — the timeline itself is a natural enough structure.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every photo sorted into a labeled album so I can find any image instantly without scrolling through hundreds of unsorted shots. Clearing out duplicates and tagging favorites now means the library stays navigable long after the trip.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: The seasons are changing and you've started pulling out sweaters and jackets from a storage bin while your summer clothes are still hanging in the closet. The closet is getting crowded with both seasons mixed together. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I hang the sweaters and jackets wherever there's space among the summer clothes. When I get dressed I'll just push hangers aside and grab what fits the weather — having both seasons accessible actually feels convenient.
scenarios.safe: I pull all the summer clothes off the hangers, fold and store them in the bin, then hang the fall and winter pieces grouped by type so the closet holds only the current season in a clear arrangement.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having everything mixed and available — some fall days are still warm, so I'd rather grab from a blended closet than commit to a seasonal swap. I'll just improvise each morning based on the forecast.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want the closet to hold only the current season's clothes, each grouped by type, so I can see every option at a glance. Storing off-season items in a labeled bin means the closet stays uncluttered and I never waste time pushing past things I won't wear.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just finished cooking a big dinner for friends. The kitchen counter is covered with cutting boards, vegetable scraps, used pans, open spice jars, and sauce-splattered utensils. Your friends are settling into the living room and calling you to join them. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I toss the perishables in the fridge, leave everything else spread across the counter, and head out to join my friends. I'll deal with it later tonight or tomorrow morning when I feel like it.
scenarios.safe: I quickly sort the dishes into the sink by type, cap and return each spice jar to its shelf spot, scrape scraps into the compost bin, and wipe down the counter before joining my friends.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like coming back to the kitchen later and just handling things as I encounter them—it feels more natural than stopping everything now to put each item back in its exact place.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every jar back on its shelf and every surface cleared now, because walking into a cluttered kitchen later means I'll have to figure out what goes where all over again.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've been building a shelf in your garage workshop all afternoon. Wood shavings, offcuts, drill bits, sandpaper sheets, and measuring tools are scattered across the workbench and floor. You're done for the day and feeling satisfied with your progress. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I set down the drill wherever there's space, leave the offcuts and shavings where they fell, and head inside. Everything's roughly where I last used it, so I'll find it again next time.
scenarios.safe: I sweep the shavings into the dustpan, sort the drill bits back into their labeled case, hang the measuring tools on their pegboard hooks, and stack the usable offcuts by size on the shelf.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer leaving the workspace in the state it naturally ends up in—tools near where I last used them, scraps nearby. I can improvise my way back into the project faster than if I reset everything.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like each tool having a fixed hook and each material having a designated bin, so when I come back I'm not searching through piles to find a specific drill bit or piece of sandpaper.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've spent the evening working on a watercolor painting at your dining table. Paint tubes are uncapped, brushes sit in murky water cups, palette trays have mixed colors drying on them, and paper towels are scattered around. You're ready to stop for the night. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I cap the tubes loosely, leave the brushes soaking, and let the palette dry as-is. Tomorrow I'll pick up right where I left off with everything still in reach.
scenarios.safe: I rinse and dry each brush, cap every tube and sort them back into the supply box by color family, clean the palette, and wipe down the table so it's clear.
scenarios.risky_thought: I enjoy having the supplies spread out in the arrangement that emerged from actually using them—it keeps the creative state alive and I can just dive back in without reconstructing my setup.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each tube capped and sorted by hue, brushes clean and upright in their holder, and the table surface clear, so tomorrow I start from a known state rather than picking through dried-out chaos.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a week-long trip. Your suitcase is open on the bedroom floor with clean clothes, dirty clothes, toiletries, chargers, and souvenirs all jumbled together. You're tired but not exhausted. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I pull out the charger I need tonight and leave the suitcase open on the floor. Over the next few days I'll grab things from it as I need them until it's basically empty.
scenarios.safe: I separate dirty clothes into the hamper, return clean clothes to their drawers, put toiletries back in the bathroom cabinet, coil chargers into the electronics drawer, and stow the empty suitcase in the closet.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer to just fish things out of the suitcase as the need arises—it's like a temporary holding zone and everything finds its way back eventually without me forcing a sorting session.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item back in its designated spot right now so the suitcase can be stored and my room returns to its normal layout without things lingering in an ambiguous pile.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: It's the end of a busy workday at your home office desk. Sticky notes are stuck to the monitor, reference printouts are fanned across the desk, pens are scattered, and two coffee mugs sit among the papers. You're about to log off. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the sticky notes where they are, let the printouts stay fanned out, and just close my laptop. Tomorrow morning I'll see exactly what I was in the middle of.
scenarios.safe: I consolidate the sticky notes onto a single strip by priority, file the printouts into their project folders, return pens to the cup, and carry the mugs to the kitchen before logging off.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like the desk reflecting the state of my thinking—the scattered notes and papers are a kind of spatial memory that helps me pick up context faster than a tidy, blank surface would.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want a cleared desk with notes sorted by priority and papers filed, so tomorrow I sit down to a surface where everything has a known location instead of having to re-read scattered fragments.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just carried in six bags of groceries and set them all on the kitchen counter and floor. The bags contain a mix of frozen items, produce, canned goods, snacks, and cleaning supplies. Your favorite show is about to start in ten minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I shove the frozen stuff into the freezer, leave the rest in the bags on the counter, and go watch my show. I'll put things away as I use them over the next day or two.
scenarios.safe: I unpack every bag, grouping items by where they go—freezer, fridge, pantry shelf, under-sink cabinet—then put each group away in its designated spot before sitting down.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer just handling the urgent stuff and leaving the rest in bags as a kind of temporary pantry—I'll naturally encounter each item when I need it and put it away then.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each item shelved in its category right now so the counter is clear and I'm not rummaging through bags later trying to remember what I bought.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: Your kids just finished a craft project at the dining table. There are glitter piles, glue sticks without caps, scissors, construction paper scraps, and finished artwork spread everywhere. The kids have run off to play outside. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I set the finished artwork aside to dry and leave the rest of the craft supplies and scraps on the table. If we do crafts again tomorrow, everything's already out and ready.
scenarios.safe: I cap the glue sticks and return them to the craft bin, sort usable paper scraps by color back into the paper drawer, sweep up the glitter, and hang the finished artwork on the drying line.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like keeping the craft stuff out in its used state—it's basically pre-staged for the next session, and the kids can just sit down and start creating without waiting for me to set everything up again.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each supply back in its labeled bin and the table surface wiped clean, so we're not hunting for a glue stick under paper scraps or accidentally glittering tomorrow's homework.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just pulled a large load of clean laundry out of the dryer and dumped it on your bed. There are shirts, pants, socks, towels, and underwear all tangled together in a warm heap. You need to leave for an errand in about 30 minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push the pile to one side of the bed and pull items from it as I need them over the next couple of days. I can always find what I'm looking for by digging through it.
scenarios.safe: I fold each category—shirts together, pants together, socks paired—and put them directly into their assigned drawer or shelf section before heading out.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer working from the pile directly—everything's clean, it's all right there, and I can grab what I need without the overhead of folding and sorting into drawers.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each piece folded and placed in its specific drawer slot now, so getting dressed tomorrow is a matter of opening a drawer, not excavating a heap.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a weekend camping trip and unloaded your car into the garage. The tent, sleeping bags, cooking gear, cooler, headlamps, and dirty boots are all piled together on the garage floor. It's Sunday evening and you have work tomorrow. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything in the pile on the garage floor. Next time I go camping, I'll sort through it and repack what I need—it's all in one spot anyway.
scenarios.safe: I air out the tent and sleeping bags, wash and dry the cooking gear, return the headlamps to the gear shelf, clean the boots, and store each item in its labeled bin on the garage rack.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like keeping the gear in its natural post-trip cluster—when I plan the next trip, I can just sift through the pile and grab what I need rather than pulling items from six different storage spots.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each piece of gear cleaned and returned to its labeled bin on the shelf, so next time I'm packing I can pull exactly what I need from a known location without sorting through a tangled pile.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You moved into your apartment three months ago and there are still two large boxes in the corner of the living room. They contain a mix of books, kitchen gadgets, photo frames, cables, and miscellaneous items. A friend mentions they're coming over next weekend. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the boxes where they are—they're not really in the way, and if I need something from them I just dig through until I find it. My friend won't care.
scenarios.safe: I unpack both boxes, shelve the books alphabetically, put kitchen gadgets in their cabinet spots, hang the photo frames, and coil the cables into the designated drawer before recycling the boxes.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like having those boxes as a kind of reserve—when I need a cable or a book, I rummage through and usually find it quickly enough. Distributing everything to permanent spots feels like unnecessary overhead.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item assigned a permanent home—books on the shelf, gadgets in the right cabinet, cables in the drawer—so I can finally recycle the boxes and stop relying on memory to know what's buried in them.
